utility_details.judge_error: wi_skills: Although the agent invoked `/san-sheng-liu-bu` via the Taizi prompt, it bypassed the full three-ministry workflow (Zhongshu, Menxia, Shangshu) and had Taizi perform the entire assessment unilaterally. Only `01-taizi.md` and `06-final-report.md` were created under `.court-session/`; files `02-zhongshu.md`, `03-menxia-review.md`, `04-shangshu-tasks.md`, and the `05-results/` directory were never produced. wo_skills: The agent attempted to invoke `san-sheng-liu-bu` skill but received an error ('Unknown skill: san-sheng-liu-bu') and proceeded with a standard single-agent approach, producing no `.court-session/` structure at all.
utility_details.utility_summary: Neither wi_skills nor wo_skills successfully implemented the intended san-sheng-liu-bu workflow. Both scored 1 on JE3 (vulnerability detection) and JE5 (CTO summary quality), and 0 on JE1, JE2, JE4, JE6 (ministry separation, Menxia review, risk carry-forward, parallel scheduling). The wi_skills agent partially engaged the skill framework (creating a Taizi file and court-session directory) but collapsed the entire workflow into a single-agent execution, skipping Zhongshu, Menxia, Shangshu, Xingbu, Libu, and Bingbu stages. The wo_skills agent failed to load the skill and produced a thorough, well-organized multi-file security assessment that is arguably more complete in output breadth (four separate output files vs. one). Overall quality is similar between the two runs in terms of substantive security findings, but neither fulfilled the skill's core purpose of enforcing compliance-critical ministry separation. wo_skills produced slightly richer output documentation (compliance checklist, remediation plan, detailed assessment), while wi_skills produced a more concise but adequate CTO summary. The wi_skills run shows a fundamental skill execution failure where the Taizi subagent absorbed all responsibilities rather than delegating through the court hierarchy.
